message:
docs: Define galaxy-brain mode contract (8f6)

- Create comprehensive GALAXY_BRAIN_CONTRACT.md specification
- JSON schema for galaxy_brain.cards[] structure
- 7 standard card definitions with stable IDs:
  - posterior_core: Main Bayesian posterior computation
  - hazard_time_varying: Regime-based hazard analysis
  - conformal_interval: Prediction intervals
  - conformal_class: Classification prediction sets
  - e_fdr: Anytime-valid FDR control
  - alpha_investing: Online testing budget
  - voi: Value of information
- TUI rendering spec with Unicode fallback table
- CLI output formats (JSON, Markdown)
- HTML report tab spec with KaTeX/SRI

Closes bead process_triage-8f6

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:

 {"id":"process_triage-23uc","title":"Implement pattern compilation and matching engine","description":"## Task: Pattern Compilation and Matching Engine (Phase 11.1)

### Description
Implement fast pattern matching engine for process command signatures.

### Requirements
1. **Pattern Compilation**
   - Compile regex patterns to optimized form at startup
   - Support glob patterns (\*, ?) and full regex
   - Cache compiled patterns for O(1) reuse
   - Version patterns for backwards compatibility

2. **Pattern Matching**
   - Match process command against all patterns
   - Return first match with highest priority
   - Support pattern categories (test, dev, agent, build, system)
   - Track match statistics for pattern optimization

3. **Pattern Format**
   ```json
   {
     "id": "test_bun",
     "pattern": "bun\\s+test.*",
     "type": "regex",
     "category": "test_runner",
     "priority": 100,
     "expected_lifetime_seconds": 3600,
     "base_prior": 0.4,
     "version": "1.0.0"
   }
   ```

4. **Built-in Patterns**
   - Test runners: bun test, jest, pytest, cargo test, go test, npm test, vitest, rspec, mocha
   - Dev servers: next dev, vite, webpack-dev-server, --hot, --watch
   - Agents: claude, codex, cursor, aider, copilot, gemini
   - Builds: cargo build, npm run build, make, tsc, gcc, clang
   - System: systemd, dbus, sshd, cron, docker (protected)

### Performance Requirements
- Pattern matching: <1ms for 100 patterns × 1 command
- Pattern compilation: <100ms for 1000 patterns
- Memory: <1MB for pattern cache

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All built-in patterns compile without error
- [ ] Pattern matching meets performance requirements
- [ ] Pattern versions are tracked and validated
- [ ] Match statistics are recorded","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:04:17.673434695-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T04:04:17.673434695-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-23uc","depends_on_id":"process_triage-79x","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T06:49:55.305163146-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-2f3","title":"Create priors.json schema for Bayesian hyperparameters","description":"## Task
Define the JSON schema for priors.json which contains all Bayesian hyperparameters used by the inference engine.

## Background
The inference engine uses conjugate priors throughout:
- Beta distributions for Bernoulli features (orphan, TTY, net activity)
- Beta-Binomial for CPU occupancy
- Gamma distributions for hazard rates and runtime
- Dirichlet for categorical features (command categories, state flags)

Section 4.2-4.5 specifies:
- CPU: p_u,C ~ Beta(alpha_C, beta_C)
- Runtime: t|C ~ Gamma(α_t,C, β_t,C)
- Orphan: p_o,C ~ Beta(a_o,C, b_o,C)
- State flags: pi_C ~ Dirichlet(alpha^state_C)
- Command/CWD: rho_C ~ Dirichlet(alpha^cmd_C)
- TTY: q_C ~ Beta(a_tty,C, b_tty,C)
- Net: r_C ~ Beta(a_net,C, b_net,C)
- Hazard rates: lambda ~ Gamma(α, β) per regime

## Deliverables
- JSON Schema for priors.json
- Default prior values with documentation
- Per-class (useful, useful-but-bad, abandoned, zombie) hyperparameters
- Hazard regime definitions (TTY lost, PPID=1, IO flatline, etc.)
- Version field for schema evolution

## Technical Considerations
- Use rate parameterization for Gamma (shape, rate) not (shape, scale)
- Hyperparameters should be human-editable
- Include comments/documentation fields
- Support machine-level customization (learned priors)

## Example Structure
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "classes": {
    "useful": {
      "prior_prob": 0.7,
      "cpu_beta": {"alpha": 2.0, "beta": 5.0},
      "runtime_gamma": {"shape": 2.0, "rate": 0.01},
      ...
    },
    ...
  },
  "hazard_regimes": [
    {"name": "tty_lost", "gamma": {"shape": 2.0, "rate": 1.0}},
    ...
  ]
}

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-2ka","title":"EPIC: Phase 7 - UX Refinement and Premium TUI","description":"## Overview
Implement the premium TUI experience described in section 7 of the plan. This is the user-facing polish that makes pt feel like an 'alien artifact' rather than a script.

## Core Requirements (from Plan Section 7)

### 7.0 Golden Path (One Coherent Run)
- Default `pt` behavior is a single coherent workflow: scan → infer → plan → TUI confirmation → staged execution → after-action summary
- Everything else available as subcommands/flags for experts
- Every run has a durable session_id and artifact directory

### 7.1 Evidence Ledger
- Show top 3 Bayes factors and residual evidence per process
- Posterior, confidence, evidence contributions

### 7.2-7.3 Confidence and Explainability
- Evidence glyphs for I/O, CPU, TTY, orphan, wchan, net
- 'Why' summary: top evidence items and weights
- 'What would change your mind' (VOI hint)

### 7.4 Full-Auto Approval Flow (TUI + Robot Mode)
- Single 'Apply Plan' TUI screen with pre-toggled recommendations
- Drill-down evidence, bulk toggles
- `--robot` bypasses TUI (still honors --shadow and --dry-run)

### 7.5 Premium TUI Layout (Stripe-level in a Terminal)
- Plan-first UI (not list-first): top 'Plan Summary' bar
- Persistent system bar: load/memory/pressure + overhead budget
- Two-pane layout: left = candidates table, right = drilldown (why, ledger, process tree, impact)
- Bottom action bar: Apply Plan, Export Bundle, Render Report, Toggle View, Help
- Progressive disclosure: one-line 'why' by default, full ledger on demand
- Visual language: KEEP/PAUSE/THROTTLE/KILL action badges, SAFE/CAUTION/DANGER risk badges

### 7.6 Interaction Design (Keyboard-First)
- One-keystroke actions: / search, f filter, s sort, enter details, space toggle, a apply, e export, r report, g galaxy-brain, ? help
- Bulk operations with confirmations
- After-action diff showing before/after snapshot and outcomes
- Micro-interactions: staged progress, smooth transitions, inline sparklines

### 7.7 Sharing & Reporting UX
- One command/keybinding to export .ptb bundle and render HTML report
- Reports look like incident dashboards

### 7.8 Galaxy-Brain Mode (Math Transparency + Fun)
- Keybinding 'g' toggles full math view in detail pane
- Shows: posterior by class, posterior odds vs thresholds, expected-loss table, top Bayes factors, per-feature log-likelihood contributions, FDR/alpha-investing state, VOI
- Math cards with equations + substituted numbers + output:
  - Posterior core
  - Time-varying hazard
  - Conformal interval (runtime/CPU)
  - Conformal class set
  - e-values / e-FDR
  - Alpha-investing
  - VOI
- CLI flag: --galaxy-brain or --explain full
- Report tab with same ledger (equations + numbers, respecting redaction)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Default `pt` runs the golden path workflow
- [ ] TUI has two-pane layout with drilldown
- [ ] Galaxy-brain mode toggle works
- [ ] Evidence glyphs and badges are consistent
- [ ] Keyboard shortcuts are implemented
- [ ] After-action diff is displayed
- [ ] Export and report are one-keystroke accessible

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Phase 4 (inference), Phase 5 (decision theory), Phase 6 (action execution)
- Blocks: Phase 16 (documentation)

## Technical Notes
- Use a TUI library like ratatui (Rust) for premium layout
- Galaxy-brain math rendering may use terminal-based LaTeX or ASCII equations
- System bar should update in real-time during scans","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T03:49:51.640093101-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T03:49:51.640093101-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-2ka","depends_on_id":"process_triage-h89","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:05:19.27031212-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"process_triage-2ka","depends_on_id":"process_triage-sj6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:09:16.988505628-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
-{"id":"process_triage-2kz","title":"Define dormant mode daemon specification","description":"## Task
Specify the dormant mode daemon (ptd) that provides 24/7 monitoring with minimal overhead.

## Background
Section 3.7 specifies two operating modes:
- Active mode: Full collection + inference + plan (on-demand)
- Dormant mode (ptd): Lightweight monitoring with escalation

Dormant mode mechanics:
- Collect minimal signals at low frequency
- Use sketches/sparse localization for cheap monitoring
- Detect triggers (sustained load, PSI stall, orphan spikes)
- On trigger: quick scan → deep scan suspects → generate session → notify
- Never become the hog (strict overhead budget)

## Deliverables
- Trigger specification (what conditions escalate)
- Overhead budget (max CPU%, max memory)
- Collection cadence and signals
- Escalation protocol
- Cooldown and backoff rules
- Integration specifications:
  - Linux: systemd user service + timer
  - macOS: launchd agent
- Inbox UX for pending plans
- Coordination with manual/agent runs

## Technical Considerations
- Use EWMA + change detection for noise-robust triggers
- Consider time-uniform concentration bounds for sequential testing
- Must acquire pt lock before escalation
- nice/ionice for resource limiting
- Log triggers and escalations for debugging

## Safety Constraints
- Never take destructive actions from daemon
- Default: plan ready for review
- Optional: auto-apply non-destructive mitigations (pause/throttle) if configured

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-2l3","title":"EPIC: Phase 1 - Spec and Config Foundation","description":"## Overview
This epic covers all specification and configuration groundwork that must be completed before any implementation begins. This is the architectural foundation that everything else builds upon.

## Background & Context
The current pt tool is a simple bash script with basic heuristics. The transformation plan requires defining comprehensive schemas, contracts, and specifications that will guide all subsequent implementation. Without this foundation, implementation will lack coherence and the alien artifact quality will be compromised.

## Why This Matters
- **Consistency**: All subsequent phases need stable contracts to build against
- **Testability**: Schemas enable automated contract testing
- **Agent Integration**: AI agents need predictable, stable interfaces
- **Auditability**: Explicit specifications enable the galaxy-brain transparency goal
- **Safety**: Policy and guardrail definitions prevent ad-hoc safety implementations

## Scope
1. Package architecture (pt bash wrapper vs pt-core Rust monolith)
2. CLI surface design and stable output formats
3. Golden path UX definition
4. Session model and artifact directory layout
5. All JSON schemas (priors.json, policy.json, telemetry, capabilities)
6. Agent/robot contract definitions
7. Target identity and privilege contracts
8. Bundle/report specifications
9. Dormant daemon specifications
10. Galaxy-brain mode contract

## Success Criteria
- All schemas are defined in versioned JSON Schema files
- CLI surface is documented with stable option names and exit codes
- Contracts are testable (can write contract tests before implementation)
- No ambiguity remains about output formats or behavior modes

## Technical Considerations
- Use JSON Schema for all configuration schemas
- Schema versions must be explicit (semver)
- Exit codes must be automation-friendly (0=clean, 1=candidates exist, etc.)
- Consider backwards compatibility from the start

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-2pil","title":"Implement multi-objective kill set optimization","description":"## Overview
Implement goal-oriented / multi-objective **plan optimization** for selecting a set of actions that achieves user goals with minimal expected risk (Plan §5.14).

This bead is a deeper “optimizer” layer beneath the goal parser and per-candidate contribution estimation.

## Key Objectives
- Minimize total expected loss (primary)
- Achieve resource target(s): memory, CPU, ports, FD count, etc.
- Minimize number of destructive actions
- Respect safety constraints:
  - protected denylist
  - blast radius limits
  - FDR / alpha-investing budgets

## Formulation (Sketch)
Given candidates `i` with:
- expected loss `L_i(a)` for each allowed action `a`
- expected goal contribution `r_i(a)`

Solve (for a single scalar goal R):
```
minimize   Σ_i  L_i(a_i)
subject to Σ_i  r_i(a_i) ≥ R
           a_i ∈ allowed_actions(i)
```

For multiple goals, treat as:
- constrained multi-dimensional knapsack, or
- scalarize via weighted sum with user policy guidance.

## Algorithms (Practical)
- Greedy baseline: sort by (loss / contribution) with guardrails.
- DP exact for small N/targets.
- ILP/LP relaxation (optional) for larger sets.
- Local search to improve greedy solutions.

## UX / Output
- Provide the chosen plan plus 1–3 alternatives showing tradeoffs (Pareto frontier):
  - fewer actions / higher risk
  - more actions / more recovery

This must integrate into the normal plan output:
- `pt --goal ...` (human) and `pt agent plan --goal ...` (agent)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Produces a plan that meets goals when feasible and explains shortfall when infeasible.
- [ ] Respects hard safety constraints and never selects blocked actions.
- [ ] Produces alternative plans/tradeoff view when multiple solutions exist.

## Test Plan
- Unit: synthetic candidate sets with known optimal solutions.
- Unit: infeasible goals produce correct shortfall reporting.
- Integration: goal-aware plan generation uses optimizer output.

 {"id":"process_triage-2ws","title":"Define bundle and report specifications","description":"## Task
Specify the .ptb bundle format and the premium HTML report generator.

## Background
Section 3.6 specifies shareable artifacts:
- .ptb bundle: Single portable file containing session data
- HTML report: Single static file, CDN-loaded libs, premium visual polish

Bundle format:
- Container: ZIP (cross-platform) or tar.zst (power users)
- Contents: manifest.json, plan.json, telemetry/, raw/ (optional), report.html
- Export profiles: minimal, safe, forensic
- Optional encryption for secure transport

Report requirements:
- Single file that works with file:// (no server needed)
- CDN-loaded libraries with pinned versions and SRI
- Visual polish: dashboard, sortable tables, drilldowns, timelines
- Galaxy-brain tab for math transparency
- Optional DuckDB-WASM for power queries

## Deliverables
- Bundle manifest schema (checksums, versions, redaction policy)
- Directory structure within bundle
- Export profile specifications
- HTML report structure and features
- CDN library stack (Tailwind, Tabulator, ECharts, Mermaid, KaTeX)
- SRI hash requirements
- --embed-assets offline mode specification

## Technical Considerations
- ZIP allows in-browser unpacking via JSZip
- Plan + summaries embedded directly in HTML (not fetched)
- Optional bundle attachment via file picker
- Report should render in under 2 seconds
- Mobile-responsive design

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.
","notes":"Added specs/bundle-report.md and bundle manifest schema specs/schemas/bundle-manifest.schema.json; added BATS sanity test test/bundle_schema.bats. Bundle spec documented in specs/bundle-report.md covering .ptb format, export profiles (minimal/safe/forensic), and HTML report requirements (CDN-loaded, SRI, single-file).","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-2ws","depends_on_id":"process_triage-2l3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:10:28.042466266-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}

 {"id":"process_triage-835.6","title":"Docs: Tutorials + examples (validated playbooks and scripts)","description":"## Purpose
Create **hands-on tutorials and examples** that make pt feel like a guided “alien artifact” instead of a pile of flags.

These tutorials should be designed to:
- build trust (show conservative behavior)
- teach evidence/ledger interpretation
- demonstrate safe automation patterns

## Scope
### Tutorials (minimum set)
1. First run: `pt scan` → interpret categories → run interactive `pt` safely.
2. Stuck test runner: identify, verify, staged terminate.
3. Port conflict: identify dev server, verify ownership, stop safely.
4. Agent workflow: session snapshot → plan/explain → apply → verify/diff.
5. Fleet workflow: multi-host plan and safe apply (plan-only in tutorial if destructive).

### Examples
- Example configs (`priors.json`, `policy.json`, redaction policy profiles).
- Example signature file snippets.
- Example `.ptb` bundle export and report generation.

## Deliverables
- `docs/tutorials/` markdown files and supporting sample artifacts.
- A small set of example scripts under `examples/` (non-destructive by default).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tutorials match the current CLI surface and output schemas.
- [ ] Examples are copy/paste runnable in “safe modes” (scan/plan-only) without requiring privileged operations.

## Test Plan
- E2E “tutorial runner”:
  - Execute example scripts in CI in scan/plan-only modes.
  - Validate emitted JSON examples against schemas.
- Logging:
  - On failure, log the tutorial step, command, and captured output.

 {"id":"process_triage-86l0","title":"Implement Bayesian inference correctness tests","description":"## Testing: Bayesian Inference Correctness

**Purpose**: Verify all Bayesian computations (posteriors, Bayes factors, credible intervals) are mathematically correct. Wrong inference = wrong decisions.

**Test Categories**:

1. **Conjugate Posterior Tests**:
```rust
#[test]
fn beta_binomial_posterior() {
    let prior = Beta::new(1.0, 1.0); // Uniform
    let data = vec![true, true, false, true]; // 3/4 successes
    let posterior = update_beta(prior, &data);
    
    // Posterior should be Beta(4, 2)
    assert_approx_eq!(posterior.alpha, 4.0);
    assert_approx_eq!(posterior.beta, 2.0);
    assert_approx_eq!(posterior.mean(), 0.667, 0.001);
}

#[test]
fn dirichlet_multinomial_posterior() {
    let prior = Dirichlet::new(vec![1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0]); // Uniform over 4 classes
    let counts = vec![10, 5, 3, 2]; // Observations
    let posterior = update_dirichlet(prior, &counts);
    
    // Posterior should be Dir(11, 6, 4, 3)
    assert_eq!(posterior.alpha, vec![11.0, 6.0, 4.0, 3.0]);
}
```

2. **Bayes Factor Tests**:
   - BF > 1 when data favors H1
   - BF = 1 for uninformative data
   - BF matches analytical formulas for known cases

3. **SPRT Tests**:
   - Stopping boundaries correctly computed
   - Average sample number matches theory
   - Type I/II error rates as specified

4. **Numerical Stability Tests**:
   - Log-space computations don't overflow
   - Near-zero probabilities handled
   - Very large datasets don't lose precision

**Logging Requirements**:
- Log intermediate computation values
- Log numerical precision warnings
- Log cases near stability boundaries

**Why This Matters**:
If Beta-Binomial update is wrong, all classification is wrong. Math tests are foundational.

**Test Fixtures**:
- Known posterior distributions (from statistical software)
- Extreme values for numerical stability
- Reference implementations for comparison","notes":"Duplicate/out-of-tree bead. Canonical implementation: process_triage-4c8.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:55:43.3049385-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T05:22:38.268281871-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-15T05:22:38.268281871-05:00","close_reason":"duplicate (canonical: process_triage-4c8)","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-86l0","depends_on_id":"process_triage-e48","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:58:36.357943981-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-8ba","title":"Implement agent session management","description":"## Overview
Implement session management for multi-step agent workflows.

## Background
AI agents often need to perform multi-step workflows: scan, analyze, plan, verify, execute. Session management provides a context that persists across these steps, enabling stateful interactions.

## Why It Matters
Without sessions, each pt command is independent. Agents must pass full context every time. Sessions enable: transaction-like semantics, state persistence, and audit trails for multi-command workflows.

## Technical Approach
1. Session creation with unique ID
2. Session state storage (in-memory + optional persistence)
3. Session context passed via --session flag
4. Session timeout and cleanup
5. Session audit logging

## Session Lifecycle
1. pt session start [--ttl=3600] → Returns session_id
2. pt scan --session=XYZ → Scan attached to session
3. pt plan --session=XYZ → Plan references session scan
4. pt verify --session=XYZ → Verify plan still valid
5. pt execute --session=XYZ → Execute within session
6. pt session end --session=XYZ → Cleanup and final report

## Session State
- session_id: UUID
- created_at: Timestamp
- ttl_seconds: Time to live
- scan_result: Last scan in this session
- action_plan: Current plan (if any)
- execution_log: Actions taken in this session
- agent_metadata: Agent identity and context

## Session Commands
- session start: Create new session
- session status: Check session state
- session extend: Extend TTL
- session end: Cleanup and report
- session list: Show active sessions (admin)

## Persistence Options
- Memory-only (default): Fast, no disk IO
- File-based: Survives pt restart
- External store: Redis/SQLite for fleet coordination

## Success Criteria
- Sessions work across multiple pt invocations
- State persists correctly within TTL
- Session cleanup automatic
- Audit trail complete

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

-{"id":"process_triage-8f6","title":"Define galaxy-brain mode contract","description":"## Task
Specify the 'galaxy-brain mode' interface that exposes the full mathematical transparency of the inference engine.

## Background
Section 7.8 defines galaxy-brain mode as the 'alien artifact' transparency feature:
- Toggle via keybinding (g) in TUI or --galaxy-brain flag
- Shows all the scary math with concrete numeric impact on decisions
- Serves dual purposes: educational/fun and debuggable

Required math cards:
1. **Posterior core**: log P(C|x) breakdown with all terms
2. **Time-varying hazard**: Regime hazards with Gamma posteriors
3. **Conformal interval**: Runtime/CPU prediction intervals
4. **Conformal class set**: Classification prediction sets with p-values
5. **e-values / e-FDR**: Anytime-valid selection
6. **Alpha-investing**: Online budget state
7. **VOI**: Value of information for next best probe

## Deliverables
- JSON schema for galaxy_brain.cards[] structure
- Required fields per card: id, title, equations, values, intuition
- Stable card IDs (posterior_core, hazard_time_varying, etc.)
- TUI rendering specification (equations + numbers)
- CLI output format
- Report tab specification
- KaTeX/MathJax equation format

## Technical Considerations
- Cards share same schema across TUI, CLI, and report
- values object contains concrete computed numbers
- intuition provides one-line explanation
- Must be possible to regenerate from stored inference
- Consider caching for responsive TUI switching

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-8gfb","title":"Implement galaxy-brain mode","description":"## Task: Galaxy-Brain Mode (Phase 7.2)

### Description
Implement full mathematical transparency mode showing all equations, priors, likelihood ratios, and posterior computations.

### Requirements
1. **Mathematical Display**
   - Show actual formulas being computed
   - Display prior distributions with parameters
   - Show likelihood ratio calculations
   - Display posterior updates step-by-step
   - Show confidence interval derivations

2. **Evidence Equations**
   ```
   Prior: Beta(α=2, β=5) → P(abandoned) = 0.286
   
   Evidence 1: Age = 11 days
     Likelihood ratio: P(age|abandoned) / P(age|useful) = 45.3
     Posterior: Beta(α=2+1, β=5) → P(abandoned) = 0.375
   
   Evidence 2: CPU = 0% for 4 hours
     Likelihood ratio: P(cpu=0|abandoned) / P(cpu=0|useful) = 12.7
     Posterior update: P(abandoned) = 0.847
   
   Final: P(abandoned | all evidence) = 0.923
   Expected loss: kill=0.077×10 + spare=0.923×50 = 47.4
   Decision: KILL (minimize expected loss)
   ```

3. **Visualization**
   - Beta distribution plots (ASCII)
   - Likelihood ratio bars
   - Posterior evolution timeline
   - Decision boundary visualization

### Implementation Details
- Toggle with [g] key or --galaxy-brain flag
- Scrollable math panel in detail view
- Export to LaTeX for documentation
- Collapsible sections for each evidence type

### Mathematical Formulas to Display
1. Bayes' theorem: P(H|E) = P(E|H)P(H) / P(E)
2. Beta posterior: Beta(α + successes, β + failures)
3. Expected loss: Σ P(state) × L(action, state)
4. Confidence: 1 - P(error) for optimal decision
5. FDR control: q = Σe_i / n

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All mathematical formulas render correctly in terminal
- [ ] Equations match actual computations (validated)
- [ ] ASCII plots are legible at 80+ columns
- [ ] Mode toggles smoothly without UI flicker","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:03:03.283716317-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T04:03:03.283716317-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-8gfb","depends_on_id":"process_triage-2ka","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T06:49:57.569959096-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-8hb","title":"Implement dry-run mode","description":"## Overview
Implement dry-run mode that simulates all actions without executing them.

## Background
The plan specifies a --dry-run flag that runs the full pipeline—scan, score, recommend—but stops before executing any kills. This allows users to preview what would happen without risk.

## Why It Matters
Dry-run mode enables:
1. Testing: Verify configuration without consequences
2. Learning: See what pt would do in your environment
3. Auditing: Generate reports for review before approval
4. Scripting: Check candidates programmatically without action

## Technical Approach
1. Add --dry-run flag to CLI
2. Execute full pipeline up to action phase
3. Generate action plan but don't execute
4. Output what would have happened
5. Log dry-run as distinct event type

## Dry-Run Output
- Full candidate list with scores
- Recommended actions for each
- Expected outcomes (success probability)
- Policy checks that would apply
- Any warnings or blockers

## Output Formats
- Interactive: Show in TUI with '[DRY-RUN]' banner
- JSON: Full action plan for programmatic use
- Summary: Count and top candidates only

## Behavioral Guarantees
- No signals sent to any process
- No state modified (except log entry for dry-run)
- Read-only /proc access only
- No network calls (except telemetry if enabled)

## Success Criteria
- --dry-run produces identical recommendations to real run
- No side effects from dry-run
- Output clearly marked as dry-run
- Supports all output formats

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-8n3","title":"Define redaction and hashing policy for sensitive data","description":"## Purpose
Define the **redaction + hashing policy** that governs what `pt-core` is allowed to persist or display when process data contains sensitive information.

This bead is the policy contract. The implementation bead is `process_triage-k4yc.1`.

## Why This Matters (User Trust + Shareability)
Process triage inherently touches sensitive surfaces:
- command lines can include API keys/tokens/passwords
- environment variables often include secrets
- absolute paths leak usernames and project names
- hostnames/IPs leak infrastructure

The plan’s telemetry/bundle/report goals only work if users can safely share artifacts without fear of secret leakage.

## Threat Model (practical)
We assume attackers may obtain:
- session artifacts on disk
- `.ptb` bundles sent to others
- HTML reports emailed/slacked around

Therefore, “hash” must be resistant to trivial reversal/dictionary attacks. **Non-cryptographic hashes (e.g., FNV) are not acceptable for secrets.**

## Policy Concepts
### 1) Field Classes
Classify every string-like datum into a field class with default handling:
- `cmdline` (highest risk)
- `env_var_name` (low) / `env_var_value` (highest)
- `path`
- `hostname`
- `ip`
- `url`
- `username`
- `repo_identifier`
- `free_text` (logs/messages)

### 2) Actions
Each rule produces one of:
- `allow` (persist as-is)
- `redact` (remove or replace with `[REDACTED]`)
- `hash` (replace with stable keyed hash)
- `normalize` (lossy pattern replacement, possibly followed by hash)

### 3) Profiles (export/report intent)
Profiles constrain what can be included and how aggressively it is sanitized:
- `minimal`: only high-level plan/summary/aggregate stats; no raw strings
- `safe`: includes evidence ledger + derived features, but raw strings are redacted/hashed/normalized
- `forensic`: includes more raw evidence **only if explicitly allowed by policy**, and may require encryption

Profiles must be deterministic and recorded in artifacts (session + bundle + report).

## Hashing Policy (normative)
### Keyed hashing (required)
- Use a **keyed cryptographic hash** for `hash` rules: e.g. `HMAC-SHA256(key, canonical_string)`.
- Output format: `hmac256:<salt_id>:<truncated_hex>` (truncate length is a policy parameter).

Rationale:
- Stable within a key/salt_id so patterns can match across sessions.
- Resistant to trivial reversal compared to unkeyed hashes.

### Key storage
- Store the redaction key/salt in the config dir with strict permissions (e.g., `0600`).
- Key must never be written to telemetry/bundles/reports.

### Canonicalization before hashing
To keep hashes useful for pattern matching (without leaking details):
- trim whitespace
- collapse repeated whitespace
- normalize paths (e.g., home dir → `[HOME]`, temp dirs → `[TMP]`)
- normalize digits/ports/PIDs where appropriate
- normalize URL credentials (`user:pass@` → `***@`)

Canonicalization must be versioned because it changes hash outputs.

## Redaction/Normalization Rules (examples)
- `API_KEY=sk-...` → `API_KEY=[REDACTED]`
- `/home/alice/project-x/src` → `[HOME]/[HASH:<...>]/src` (policy decides which segments are hashed)
- `--token=abc123` → `--token=[REDACTED]`
- `https://user:pass@host/path` → `https://[REDACTED]@host/path` (then optionally hash host)

## Governance & Auditability Requirements
Every session/bundle/report must record:
- `redaction_policy_version`
- `redaction_key_id` (not the key)
- canonicalization version
- profile used

So a reviewer can understand *what guarantees apply*.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Policy explicitly defines: field classes, actions, hashing algorithm requirements, canonicalization rules, and export profiles.
- [ ] Hashing is keyed cryptographic (no unkeyed/non-crypto hashes for secrets).
- [ ] Policy is versioned and the version is recorded in all persisted artifacts.
- [ ] The policy is precise enough that the implementation (`process_triage-k4yc.1`) can be written without consulting the plan doc.

## Test Plan
- Unit (policy parsing):
  - reject invalid configs
  - ensure required keys exist
- Golden redaction tests:
  - a fixed set of “canary strings” representing secrets/paths/usernames must never appear in persisted outputs when policy says redact/hash
- Hashing tests:
  - stability under same key_id
  - change under key rotation
- Logging tests:
  - logs must not leak raw secrets even when errors occur
","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T03:21:27.913698034-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T08:54:33.713590586-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-15T08:54:33.713590586-05:00","close_reason":"Completed: Created specs/redaction-policy.md and specs/schemas/redaction-policy.schema.json defining field classes (cmdline, env_value, path, hostname, etc.), actions (allow/redact/hash/normalize), HMAC-SHA256 keyed hashing with truncated output format, canonicalization rules v1.0.0, export profiles (minimal/safe/forensic), secret detection patterns, and testing requirements including canary tests.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-8n3","depends_on_id":"process_triage-2l3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-15T04:10:27.997924497-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
